elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we consider asynchronous federated learning (FL) over time-division multiple access (TDMA)-based communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Considering TDMA for transmitting local updates can introduce significant delays to conventional synchronous FL, where all devices start local training from a common global model.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the proposed asynchronous FL approach, we partition devices into multiple TDMA groups, enabling simultaneous local computation and communication across different groups.
elements.sent-2411-004: This enhances time efficiency at the expense of staleness of local updates.
elements.sent-2411-005: We derive the relationship between the staleness of local updates and the size of the TDMA group in a training round.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, our convergence analysis shows that although outdated local updates hinder appropriate global model updates, asynchronous FL over the TDMA channel converges even in the presence of data heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, the analysis identifies the impact of outdated local updates on convergence rate.
elements.sent-2411-008: Based on observations from our convergence rate, we refine asynchronous FL strategy by introducing an intentional delay in local training.
elements.sent-2411-009: This refinement accelerates the convergence by reducing the staleness of local updates.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our extensive simulation results demonstrate that asynchronous FL with the intentional delay can rapidly reduce global loss by lowering the staleness of local updates in resource-limited wireless communication networks.
elements.sent-2411-011: The rapid proliferation of edge devices, such as Internet of Things (IoT) devices, has led to the generation of vast amounts of data.
elements.sent-2411-012: As the performance of machine learning models, particularly those involving artificial intelligence (AI), is highly dependent on the size of the datasets used during the training phase, the enormous datasets generated by these edge devices are invaluable to organizations operating AI-driven applications.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, data generated by edge devices often contains sensitive personal information, such as health or financial data, which raises significant privacy concerns.
elements.sent-2411-014: Additionally, the collection and centralized processing of such large datasets can incur substantial costs.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address these challenges, federated learning (FL) has emerged as a promising solution among researchers \cite{pmlr-v54-mcmahan17a .}
elements.sent-2411-016: An FL system typically consists of a server and multiple edge devices.
elements.sent-2411-017: In FL, direct access to the distributed local data by the server or other devices is restricted.
elements.sent-2411-018: Instead, each device updates its model locally using its own data.
elements.sent-2411-019: These local updates are then sent to the server, where they are aggregated to perform a global update.
elements.sent-2411-020: The updated global model is subsequently sent back to the devices for next round.
elements.sent-2411-021: Because FL can improve a model without requiring centralized access to all data, communication between edge devices and the server is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, communication becomes a dominant process in the overall procedure of the FL system, often serving as the bottleneck \cite{konevcny2016federated .}
elements.sent-2411-023: To address this bottleneck, several studies have explored communication-efficient FL strategies.
elements.sent-2411-024: One such approach, introduced in \cite{stich2018local, yu2019parallel , employs local stochastic gradient descent (SGD), where multiple updates are performed on the devices before reporting them to the server, thereby reducing communication frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Other approaches, such as using quantization techniques to reduce the size of communication payloads, have been proposed in \cite{reisizadeh2020fedpaq .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In \cite{pmlr-v54-mcmahan17a , federated learning strategy which selects a subset of clients is proposed to reduce communication cost.}
elements.sent-2411-027: To further accelerate convergence, researchers in \cite{shi2022talk, song2024optimal explored dynamically adjusting batch sizes during FL.}
elements.sent-2411-028: When FL is conducted over wireless channels, a proper allocation of communication resource can also improve communication efficiency of FL.
elements.sent-2411-029: Several studies have discussed energy consumption \cite{chen2020joint , number of devices \cite{song2020wireless , device scheduling \cite{yang2019scheduling , bandwidth and power allocation \cite{ji2023joint, hong2023ICC, hong2023TWC for resource-efficient FL over wireless networks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In particular, training time is heavily dependent on the multiple access scheme used for local update aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-031: Various multiple access schemes and their impact on training time were studied, including frequency-division multiple access (FDMA) \cite{shi2022talk, chen2020joint , time-division multiple access (TDMA) \cite{xu2024latency, hu2020device, bouzinis2023wireless , and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) \cite{da2022multichannel, sun2020adaptive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, in the aforementioned works, all devices are required to be synchronized; which starts local training using the shared global model at the end of previous training round.
elements.sent-2411-033: Moreover, devices communicates when all devices have completed local training.
elements.sent-2411-034: Thereby, in each training round, computation and communications are strictly divided.
elements.sent-2411-035: When devices have different computation load or capability, devices which finish local computation early need to wait for other devices often called as stragglers to keep synchronicity.
elements.sent-2411-036: To resolve straggler problem, some studies have proposed asynchronous FL \cite{lian2018asynchronous, koloskova2022sharper, wang2022asynchronous, hu2023scheduling .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In \cite{lian2018asynchronous , asynchronous SGD is shown to achieve the same convergence rate of synchronous SGD given that the maximum asynchronicity is bounded.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The effect of asynchronicity on the convergence rate was discussed in \cite{koloskova2022sharper .}
elements.sent-2411-039: When devices can select to use the most recent global model or global model received in the past, a decision policy was proposed in \cite{wang2022asynchronous .}
elements.sent-2411-040: When global model is periodically aggregated from devices which complete their local updates only, scheduling policy dependent on wireless channel and training data distribution was proposed in \cite{hu2023scheduling .}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, the impact of the communication protocol on asynchronous FL has not yet been figured out.
elements.sent-2411-042: In fact, in asynchronous FL, the convergence speed depends on the level of asynchronicity between models that devices and the server have.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, the asynchronicity is determined by the communication frequency of each device, which is affected by communication scheme critically.
elements.sent-2411-044: To address this, in this paper, we study the impact of TDMA on asynchronous FL where large number of IoT devices hold heterogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-045: To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first to consider asynchronous FL over TDMA channel.
elements.sent-2411-046: In asynchronous FL, global model can be updated while some of device are performing local training.
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, there is a difference between the shared global model used for local training and the global model to which the local update is applied.
elements.sent-2411-048: In other words, somewhat outdated local updates are used to update the global model in our asynchronous FL.
elements.sent-2411-049: The staleness of local updates depends on the number of global updates occurred after the device has received global model from the server most recently.
elements.sent-2411-050: When TDMA is used for asynchronous FL, we first demonstrate that the size of the communication group directly affects the timeliness of local updates.
elements.sent-2411-051: Furthermore, we analyze the convergence rate of asynchronous FL with outdated local updates, deriving how outdated local updates negatively impact convergence.
elements.sent-2411-052: To mitigate this, we propose a strategy that introduces an intentional delay for receiving the global model to minimize staleness without additional cost.
elements.sent-2411-053: In short, our contribution can be summarized as four-fold.
elements.sent-2411-054: We investigate asynchronous FL, which allows devices to perform local training while others communicate, enabling gains from parallel processing of communication and computation, as well as the benefits of distributed computing across multiple devices.
elements.sent-2411-055: When asynchronous FL is performed using stochastic gradient with constant delay under heterogeneous data distribution, we derive the closed-form expression of convergence rate in terms of the delay of stochastic gradient.
elements.sent-2411-056: To mitigate staleness of local updates, we propose a strategy which imposes an intentional delay on global model reception, ensuring local training starts from the latest global model as possible.
elements.sent-2411-057: We show that this approach effectively reduces update errors from outdated local updates and accelerates convergence.
elements.sent-2411-058: Through extensive experiments on real-world datasets, we demonstrate the impact of delay and the performance gains of the proposed strategy across different TDMA group sizes.
elements.sent-2411-059: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-060: In Section \ref{sec:system_model , we describe the system model considered in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The details of asynchronous FL over TDMA channel are presented in Section \ref{sec:asynch .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Section \ref{sec:convergence provides convergence analysis of asynchronous FL with outdated local updates.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Based on the convergence analysis, we propose the asynchronous FL using intentional delay in Section \ref{sec:int_delay .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Moreover, we present results of experiment using real dataset in Section \ref{sec:exp .}

elements.sent-2411-066: We consider FL over a wireless network which consists of a server and $N$ IoT devices indexed by $n \in \mathcal{N = \left\lbrace 1,2,\dots, N \right\rbrace$ as shown in the Fig \ref{fig:system_model .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The objective of the FL is to obtain a global model parameterized by a $m$-dimensional real-valued vector $\mathbf{w}$ which fits to distributive dataset across $N$ IoT devices within finite time.
elements.sent-2411-068: Each IoT device $n$ holds private local data $\mathcal{D _n$ with size $D_n = |\mathcal{D _n|$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The entire dataset stored in $N$ IoT devices is represented as $\mathcal{D = \cup_{n=1 ^N \mathcal{D _n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Similarly, we represent $D = \sum_{n=1 ^N D_n$ as total number of data samples used in FL.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Given that a global model is parameterized by a vector $\mathbf{w}$ , global loss function is defined as \begin{equation}f(w ) = \frac{1}{D} \sum_{\zeta \in D} l (w, \zeta),\end{equation} where $\zeta$ is a data sample and $l(\mathbf{w , \zeta)$ is a loss function defined for each data sample.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Moreover, the local loss function of IoT device $n$ can be written as \begin{equation}f_n(w ) = \frac{1}{D_n} \sum_{\zeta \in D_n} l ( w, \zeta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-073: Ultimately, FL aims to derive the optimal global model that minimizes the global loss function \begin{equation}w^* = \argmin_{w \in R^m} f(w ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: In general, FL iteratively follows the procedures of local training, local update transmission, global model update, and global model distribution.
elements.sent-2411-075: First, in the local training, IoT devices perform stochastic gradient descent based on its local dataset.
elements.sent-2411-076: We denote a set of IoT devices performing local training in training round $k$ as $\mathcal{C _k$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Based on the most recently received global model, IoT device $n\in\mathcal{C _k$ computes gradient using a mini-batch $\mathcal{B _{n,k}$ of data samples randomly selected from its local dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The mini-batch size is assumed to be identical to all IoT device and fixed across all training rounds, $|\mathcal{B _{n,k | = B$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Suppose each IoT device performs $H$ stochastic gradient step.
elements.sent-2411-080: Given that time is slotted and IoT devices are capable of processing $q$ data samples per time slot, the time duration for local training becomes \begin{equation}\taucomp = \left\lceil \frac{H B}{q} \right\rceil. \label{eq:t_comp}\end{equation} When IoT devices complete local training, they are ready to transmit the local updates.
elements.sent-2411-081: The set of IoT devices ready for transmission in training round $k$ is denoted as $\mathcal{N ^{\text{avail _k$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: TDMA is considered for transmitting local updates in bandwidth-limited scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-083: In each time slot, an IoT device from $\mathcal{N ^{\text{avail _k$ will transmits its local updates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: If all IoT devices are not ready for transmission, the server waits until any device completes its training.
elements.sent-2411-085: On the other hand, when multiple IoT devices are ready for transmission, the device which finishes local computation earliest transmits, breaking ties based on their indexes.
elements.sent-2411-086: In each training round, the server receives local updates from $S$ IoT devices via TDMA to update the global model.
elements.sent-2411-087: The set of IoT devices which send local updates in training round $k$ is denoted as $\mathcal{S _k\in\mathcal{N ^{\text{avail _k$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: After receiving the local updates, the server updates the global model using the aggregated local update.
elements.sent-2411-089: Let $\bar{\mathbf{g _k$ denote the aggregated local update in training round $k$.}}

elements.sent-2411-094: However, in practical scenarios, we have a deadline at which we need to finish training.

elements.sent-2411-097: In a vanilla FL setup, the local training of devices begins from the same global model which the server delivers at the end of the previous training round.
elements.sent-2411-098: If the global model in training round $k$ is represented as $\mathbf{w _k$, the aggregated local update in training round $k$ is represented as \begin{equation}\bar{g}_k= \frac{1}{S} \sum_{n\inS_k} \nabla f_n\left( w_k ; B_{n,k} \right), \label{eq:syn_AggUpdate}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{B _{n,k}$ denotes the mini-batch of device $n$ in training round $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Due to synchronicity, computations and communications can be strictly divided.
elements.sent-2411-100: Hence, the number of time slots for each training round in synchronous FL becomes \begin{equation}\tau^syn = \tau^comp + \tau^comm.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since all devices perform local training simultaneously, no transmissions occur during these time slots, leading to a waste of communication resources.
elements.sent-2411-102: Furthermore, this inefficiency becomes more severe in scenarios where local training times are prolonged due to the low computational power of IoT devices.
elements.sent-2411-103: To mitigate this inefficiency in practical systems, we propose asynchronous FL, which fully utilizes wireless channels through the introduction of asynchronous local updates.
elements.sent-2411-104: Specifically, while IoT device $n\in\mathcal{S _{k}$ communicates with the server, the other devices can compute their local updates.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Consequently, in asynchronous FL, computations and communications can be carried out within the same time slot by different groups of devices.

elements.sent-2411-162: Since the staleness of local updates is given by $G-1$ for training rounds $k\ge G$, a large number of groups implies that local updates are significantly outdated.
elements.sent-2411-163: Intuitively, a larger delay suggests that the model used to compute the stochastic gradient is likely to differ more from the model to which the gradient is applied.
elements.sent-2411-164: As a result, the stochastic gradient computed from a past global model may deviate significantly from the true gradient of the current global model, with the extent of this deviation increasing as $G$ becomes larger.
elements.sent-2411-165: Since $G$ is equal to $\frac{N {S}$, increasing the TDMA group size reduces the outdatedness.}
elements.sent-2411-166: However, since a training round ends only after $S$ IoT devices have sequentially transmitted their local updates, the number of time slots required for a singe update of global model can become excessive when $S$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-167: Therefore, in practical scenarios with limited training time, it is important to balance both reducing the outdatedness of local updates and increasing the frequency of global model updates.
elements.sent-2411-168: To achieve faster and more stable learning, we propose intentional delay FL (IDFL) which introduces an additional delay to receive a more recent global model before computing the gradient, as described in Algorithm \ref{alg:IDFL .}
elements.sent-2411-169: In conventional FL, each device receives the updated global model at the end of the same training round in which the device transmitted local update.
elements.sent-2411-170: On the other hand, in IDFL, each device waits for a predefined number of training rounds, referred to as an intentional delay to reduce the staleness of local update by receiving a more recent global model.
elements.sent-2411-171: Specifically, the IoT devices that transmitted their local updates in training round $k$ receive the updated global model in training round $k+\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Then, \eqref{eq:def_k_tilde can be rewritten as \begin{equation}\hat{k}_n =  0 k\le G-\alpha k-G+\alpha G-\alpha<k\le G \max \left\lbrace k' < k| n \in S_{k'} \right\rbrace +\alpha+ 1  otherwise .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-173: To minimize the staleness of local updates while preserving the parallel execution of computation and communication in asynchronous FL, the intentional delay needs to be carefully chosen.
elements.sent-2411-174: The following proposition provides guidance on selecting the intentional delay that minimizes the staleness of local updates without compromising the benefits of asynchronous FL.
elements.sent-2411-175: Assuming $N$ is divisible by $S$, the intentional delay $\alpha$ which minimizes the staleness of local updates without increasing the number of time slots for each training round is given by \begin{equation}\alpha =  0  if  \frac{\taucomp}{r} \geq (G-1)(S+1) G - d^* - 1  if  \frac{\taucomp}{r} < (G-1)(S+1)  , \label{eq:max_delay}\end{equation} where $d^*$ is an integer which satisfies \begin{equation}(d^* -1)(S+1) < \frac{\taucomp}{r} \leq d^* (S+1). \label{ineq:ratio}\end{equation} \begin{IEEEproof As $N$ is divisible by $S$, we have $N=GS$.}

elements.sent-2411-242: In this paper, we have investigated asynchronous FL in which the local updates of IoT devices are collected using a TDMA scheme.
elements.sent-2411-243: We have formulated that the staleness of local updates is determined by the TDMA group size and discovered the trade-off between the staleness of local updates and the frequency of global model updates in asynchronous FL over a TDMA channel.
elements.sent-2411-244: Moreover, through convergence analysis, the impact of the outdated local updates on the convergence have been derived.
elements.sent-2411-245: In order to reduce performance degradation from the outdated local updates, we have refined the FL strategy by introducing an intentional delay defined as the number of training rounds to wait for receiving more recent global model.
elements.sent-2411-246: Since the value of intentional delay is dependent on the size of TDMA group, the optimal group size is changed after intentional delay is applied for asynchronous FL over TDMA channel.
elements.sent-2411-247: In general, when the intentional delay is applied, lower size of TDMA group achieves the minimum global loss.
